You said: Hi Claude, I have started ChordLab app (based in parts on previous chats), so thanks for earlier help.

Hi Claude, I have started ChordLab app (based in parts on previous chats), so thanks for earlier help. 1:01 pm

Claude responded: Let me read the truncated middle section: I now have a clear picture of the crash.

Reading the uploaded source file

Let me read the truncated middle section:

Viewed 2 files

I now have a clear picture of the crash. Let me check a couple more things to be thorough:

Check the setupToolBar for the duplicate toolbar issue and the clicked signal double-connection

I've found four bugs — one is the direct crash cause, two are secondary crashes waiting to happen, and one is a functional problem. Here's a full breakdown:

🔴 Bug #1 — The Crash: Double clicked Signal Connection

In your constructor (lines 340–350 and 371), you connect clicked twice to the same view:

cpp
// Connection 1 (line 340) — lambda that calls loadSongQuietly + markAsPlayed
connect(m_setlistView, &QTreeView::clicked, this, [this](const QModelIndex &index) {
    ...loadSongQuietly(path); markAsPlayed(index);
});

// Connection 2 (line 371) — named slot handleSetlistItemClicked
connect(m_setlistView, &QTreeView::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleSetlistItemClicked);

Qt honours both. So a single click fires loadSongQuietly twice (once from each handler). That's your crash on double-click: a double-click emits clicked + doubleClicked. With two clicked connections, the song loader runs twice in rapid succession before the first has finished, corrupting state.

Fix: Remove the lambda connection entirely (lines 340–350). handleSetlistItemClicked already does the same job properly.

cpp
// DELETE this whole block:
connect(m_setlistView, &QTreeView::clicked, this, [this](const QModelIndex &index) {
    ...
});

🔴 Bug #2 — Crash Waiting to Happen: delete m_spacerAction in handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked

In handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked (line 749):

cpp
delete m_spacerAction;
m_spacerAction = nullptr;

You delete the action object, but m_settingsToolBar->removeAction() is called after the delete on lines 752–754. More critically, m_spacerAction is declared as a raw QAction* member but is only ever assigned in onHamburgerClicked if a spacer is added there — looking at that function, no spacer is ever added to m_settingsToolBar. So m_spacerAction is likely uninitialised or dangling when this code runs, making the delete undefined behaviour.

Fix: Since no spacer is added in onHamburgerClicked, remove the m_spacerAction block from handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked entirely, and initialise it to nullptr in the constructor to be safe.

🟡 Bug #3 — Two Toolbar Objects, One is Orphaned

In setupToolBar() (lines 539–543):

cpp
QToolBar *settingsToolBar = addToolBar("Critical Settings");  // local — used below
settingsToolBar->setObjectName("criticalSettingsToolBar");

m_settingsToolBar = addToolBar("Critical Settings");          // member — used in hamburger
m_settingsToolBar->setObjectName("criticalSettingsToolBar");  // SAME objectName!

Two toolbars are created with the same name. The m_btnToggleSetlist and other widgets are added to settingsToolBar (the local), while onHamburgerClicked adds/removes actions from m_settingsToolBar (the member). These are different objects. The setlist toolbar actions appear/disappear on the wrong toolbar, or not at all.

Fix: Delete the local settingsToolBar declaration and use m_settingsToolBar throughout setupToolBar():

cpp
m_settingsToolBar = addToolBar("Settings");
m_settingsToolBar->setObjectName("criticalSettingsToolBar");
// ... use m_settingsToolBar everywhere below

🟡 Bug #4 — loadSetFile Calls beginInsertRows/endInsertRows Around a clear()

In onLoadSetlistTriggered(), you call m_setlistManager->clear() on line 788, then loadSetFile() for each file. Inside loadSetFile, there's a beginInsertRows(QModelIndex(), 0, 0) before reading the file — but if the file fails to open, the function returns without calling endInsertRows(), leaving the model in a broken signalling state.

Fix: Move endInsertRows() to match every early return path, or better yet, remove the beginInsertRows/endInsertRows pair entirely since appendRow() already handles model signals internally:

cpp
// Remove these two lines from loadSetFile:
beginInsertRows(QModelIndex(), 0, 0);
// ...
endInsertRows();

Summary Table

#SeverityLocationIssue
1🔴 CrashConstructor lines 340–372Duplicate clicked signal connection causes double-load on any click, corrupting state on double-click
2🔴 CrashhandleSetlistItemDoubleClicked line 749delete m_spacerAction on an uninitialised/dangling pointer
3🟡 Logic bugsetupToolBar() lines 539–543Two separate toolbar objects created; hamburger actions go to the wrong one
4🟡 Model bugloadSetFileUnbalanced beginInsertRows/endInsertRows on early return

Bug #1 is almost certainly your immediate crash — fix that first and the double-click behaviour should stabilise immediately.

1:19 pm

Claude responded: Looking at the updated code, I can see the toolbar problem clearly.

Looking at the updated code, I can see the toolbar problem clearly. There are actually two issues working together:

🔴 Issue 1 — The container widget is never added to m_settingsToolBar

In setupToolBar(), you build a container widget with all your buttons (Fn keys, audio buttons, Mode toggle, Theme, Transpose, etc.) but the line that adds it to the toolbar was commented out and never replaced:

cpp
//    settingsToolBar->addWidget(container);   // ← old, commented out

And there's no m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(container); anywhere. So the entire button row is constructed but orphaned — it has no parent toolbar. It likely shows up as a floating/detached widget or just disappears. Add this line right after the connect calls at the bottom of setupToolBar():

cpp
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(container);   // ← add this

🟡 Issue 2 — The orphaned first settingsToolBar (local variable) still exists

You still have:

cpp
QToolBar *settingsToolBar = addToolBar("Critical Settings");  // local — never used now
m_settingsToolBar = addToolBar("Critical Settings");           // member — the real one

The local settingsToolBar creates a second empty toolbar on screen taking up space. Since m_btnToggleSetlist is now correctly added to m_settingsToolBar, just delete the first line entirely:

cpp
// DELETE this line:
QToolBar *settingsToolBar = addToolBar("Critical Settings");

The Fix in Context

Your setupToolBar() end should look like this:

cpp
    connect(m_btnTransposeUp,   &QPushButton::clicked, this, [=](){ shiftTransposition(1); });
    connect(m_btnTransposeDown, &QPushButton::clicked, this, [=](){ shiftTransposition(-1); });
    connect(m_btnTheme,         &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::toggleTheme);

    m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(container);  // ← THIS was missing
}

That one missing line is why your entire Edit/Play toolbar strip vanishes — the container holding all those buttons was being built but never attached to anything visible.

Logs are: 01:29:34: Starting D:\Qt\projects\ChordLabV002A\build\Desktop_Qt_6_11_1_MinGW_64_bit-Debug\ChordLabV002A.exe... qt.multimedia.ffmpeg: Using Qt multimedia with FFmpeg version 7.1.3 LGPL version 2.1 or later --> top of onLoadSetlistTriggered() Path exists? true Files found: QList("Monday Songbook 1.set", "Monday Songbook 2.set") --> "Albatross/Albatross - Fleetwood Mac[E 100bpm]" --> "Yellow Submarine/MSB 1.1 The Beatles - Yellow Submarine [G 108bpm]retuned" --> "Autumn leaves/JGP 1.1 Autumn leaves" --> "Autumn leaves/JGP 1.1 Autumn leaves_backing" --> "Blue bossa/JGP 1.2 Blue bossa" --> "Blue bossa/JGP 1.2 Blue bossa_backing" --> "Crossroads/Crossroads - Cream" --> "Crossroads/Crossroads - Cream(wTab)" --> "MSB 1/MSB 1.1 Yellow submarine [G 108 bpm]" --> "MSB 1/MSB 1.2 Irish heartbeat [G 100 bpm]" --> "MSB 1/MSB 1.3 Saltwater cowboy [D 95 bpm]" --> "MSB 1/MSB 1.4 Take me home country roads [G 84 bpm]" --> "MSB 1/MSB 1.5 The house of the rising sun [Am 110 bpm]" --> "MSB 1/MSB 1.6 Norwegian wood [D 91 bpm]" --> "MSB 1/MSB 1.7 I am Australian [C 115 bpm]" --> "MSB 1/MSB 1.8 True Blue [G 115 bpm]" --> "MSB 1/MSB 1.9 Sloop John B [E 125 bpm]" --> "MSB 1/MSB 1.10 Blowin' in the wind [D 90 bpm]" --> "MSB 2/MSB 2.1 Yesterday [F 92 bpm]" --> "MSB 2/MSB 2.2 Brown eyed girl [G 139 bpm]" --> "MSB 2/MSB 2.3 Bad moon rising [D 89 bpm]" --> "MSB 2/MSB 2.4 Sultans of swing [Dm 147 bpm]" --> "MSB 2/MSB 2.5 Sound of silence [Dm 90 bpm]" --> "MSB 2/MSB 2.6 Proud Mary [D 118 bpm]" --> "MSB 2/MSB 2.7 Let it be [C 72 bpm]" --> "MSB 2/MSB 2.8 Dirty old town [F 122 bpm]" --> "MSB 2/MSB 2.9 Day tripper [E 138 bpm]" --> "MSB 2/MSB 2.10 Wish you were here [G 112 bpm]" Available resources at :/resources/styles: QList("Adaptic(default).qss", "Darkeum(dark).qss", "Dtor(light).qss", "ExcelOffice(green).qss", "Fibers(red).qss", "Irrorater(colourful).qss") 01:29:42: The command "D:\Qt\projects\ChordLabV002A\build\Desktop_Qt_6_11_1_MinGW_64_bit-Debug\ChordLabV002A.exe" terminated abnormally.

1:28 pm

Claude responded: I can see the crash immediately from the logs.

I can see the crash immediately from the logs. It dies after the setlists load and styles are found, but before the window ever appears — right in the middle of setupToolBar(). And the culprit is right at the top of your updated function:

cpp
void MainWindow::setupToolBar() {
//    QToolBar *settingsToolBar = addToolBar("Critical Settings");
    m_settingsToolBar->setObjectName("criticalSettingsToolBar");  // ← 💥 CRASH HERE

    m_settingsToolBar = addToolBar("Critical Settings");           // ← too late!
    m_settingsToolBar->setObjectName("criticalSettingsToolBar");

You're calling m_settingsToolBar->setObjectName(...) before m_settingsToolBar has been assigned anything. It's still a null/uninitialised pointer at that point. The addToolBar call that actually creates it comes two lines later — so the first setObjectName dereferences a garbage pointer and crashes.

The fix is simple — delete that first orphaned line:

cpp
void MainWindow::setupToolBar() {
    // DELETE this line: m_settingsToolBar->setObjectName("criticalSettingsToolBar");

    m_settingsToolBar = addToolBar("Critical Settings");   // ← assign FIRST
    m_settingsToolBar->setObjectName("criticalSettingsToolBar");

    // ... rest of function

That's the only change needed. The addToolBar + setObjectName pair on lines 3-4 already does everything correctly — that first dangling call was an editing artifact from the refactor and just needs to go.

Can you discuss how Qt6 might allow the setlist/piece modifier buttons might be inserted next to the "Setlist" button - it is a little weird to have them added the end... this was by evolution rather than design :-( thanks

1:37 pm

Claude responded: Great question — this is a classic Qt toolbar layout design problem.

Great question — this is a classic Qt toolbar layout design problem. Here are your main options:

The Core Problem

QToolBar adds widgets/actions sequentially, so addAction always appends to the end. The hamburger button goes in early, but the setlist actions get added later via onHamburgerClicked, so they always land at the tail.

Option 1 — Pre-insert a Dedicated "Setlist Zone" Container (Recommended)

Instead of dynamically adding/removing actions, create a small QWidget container that sits permanently right next to the Setlist button, and just show/hide its contents:

cpp
// In setupToolBar(), right after adding m_btnToggleSetlist:
m_setlistActionsWidget = new QWidget(this);  // add as member in .h
QHBoxLayout *slLayout = new QHBoxLayout(m_setlistActionsWidget);
slLayout->setContentsMargins(0, 0, 0, 0);
slLayout->setSpacing(4);

// Move your action buttons into this container as real QPushButtons
m_btnAddSong    = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/add_sg.png"), "", this);
m_btnRemoveSong = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/remove_sg.png"), "", this);
m_btnSaveSetlist= new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/save_sl.png"), "", this);

slLayout->addWidget(m_btnAddSong);
slLayout->addWidget(m_btnRemoveSong);
slLayout->addWidget(m_btnSaveSetlist);

m_setlistActionsWidget->hide();  // hidden by default
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_setlistActionsWidget);  // inserted RIGHT HERE, next to hamburger

// Then add a separator before the rest of the toolbar
m_settingsToolBar->addSeparator();
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(container);  // your existing buttons container

Then onHamburgerClicked becomes clean — just show/hide, no add/remove:

cpp
void MainWindow::onHamburgerClicked() {
    if (m_setlistView->isHidden()) {
        m_setlistView->show();
        mainSplitter->setSizes({250, this->width() - 250});
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("× Close Setlist");
        m_setlistActionsWidget->show();   // ← just show
    } else {
        m_setlistView->hide();
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("≡ Setlist");
        m_setlistActionsWidget->hide();   // ← just hide
    }
}

And handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked similarly just calls m_setlistActionsWidget->hide() — no more removeAction calls to worry about.

This is the cleanest approach because the layout never actually changes — no add/remove thrashing, no ordering surprises, and it's trivially extensible.

Option 2 — Use insertWidget with a Stored Reference

If you want to keep QActions rather than QPushButtons, QToolBar has insertAction and insertWidget which take a before reference:

cpp
// Store a reference action marking the insertion point
m_separatorAfterSetlist = m_settingsToolBar->addSeparator();  // member QAction*

// Then when showing:
m_settingsToolBar->insertAction(m_separatorAfterSetlist, m_actAddSong);
m_settingsToolBar->insertAction(m_separatorAfterSetlist, m_actRemoveSong);
m_settingsToolBar->insertAction(m_separatorAfterSetlist, m_actSaveSetlist);

This works but is more fragile — you have to manage the reference point carefully, and insertAction on an already-present action silently moves it, which can be confusing.

Option 3 — One Unified Container for the Entire Left Zone

Take it further and wrap both the hamburger and its companion buttons into a single left-zone container from the start:

cpp
QWidget *setlistZone = new QWidget();
QHBoxLayout *szLayout = new QHBoxLayout(setlistZone);
szLayout->setContentsMargins(0,0,0,0);
szLayout->setSpacing(4);

szLayout->addWidget(m_btnToggleSetlist);
szLayout->addWidget(m_btnAddSong);    // always in layout,
szLayout->addWidget(m_btnRemoveSong); // just hidden
szLayout->addWidget(m_btnSaveSetlist);

m_btnAddSong->hide();
m_btnRemoveSong->hide();
m_btnSaveSetlist->hide();

m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(setlistZone);
m_settingsToolBar->addSeparator();
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(container);  // rest of toolbar

Recommendation

Option 1 is the sweet spot for your codebase — it fixes the ordering cleanly, simplifies onHamburgerClicked and handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked considerably, and replaces the fragile add/remove pattern with a simple show/hide. The .h changes are minimal: swap three QAction* members for three QPushButton* members plus one QWidget* m_setlistActionsWidget.

You said: Thanks, I reckon I'll try the first option.

1:39 pm

Claude responded: Sounds good!

Sounds good! Take your time — it's a satisfying refactor when it clicks into place. The show/hide approach is much more Qt-idiomatic than the add/remove dance, and your handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked will feel a lot cleaner too.

🎸 Back when you're ready!

You said: Hi Claude, I've looking at Option 1 - can you suggest the cleanest/best way to go about that please?

Hi Claude, I've looking at Option 1 - can you suggest the cleanest/best way to go about that please?

2:06 pm

Claude responded: Sure!

Sure! Here's a clean step-by-step implementation. I'll show you exactly what changes where:

Step 1 — Update mainwindow.h

Replace the three QAction* setlist members with QPushButton* equivalents and add the container widget:

cpp
// REMOVE these three:
// QAction *m_actAddSong;
// QAction *m_actRemoveSong;
// QAction *m_actSaveSetlist;
// QAction *m_spacerAction;

// ADD these:
QWidget     *m_setlistActionsWidget;
QPushButton *m_btnAddSong;
QPushButton *m_btnRemoveSong;
QPushButton *m_btnSaveSetlist;

Step 2 — Rewrite the setlist zone in setupToolBar()

Replace the current action creation block with this, inserted right after m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_btnToggleSetlist):

cpp
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_btnToggleSetlist);

// --- Setlist companion buttons (hidden until hamburger opens) ---
m_setlistActionsWidget = new QWidget(this);
QHBoxLayout *slLayout = new QHBoxLayout(m_setlistActionsWidget);
slLayout->setContentsMargins(2, 0, 2, 0);
slLayout->setSpacing(4);

m_btnAddSong     = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/add_sg.png"), "Add", this);
m_btnRemoveSong  = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/remove_sg.png"), "Remove", this);
m_btnSaveSetlist = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/save_sl.png"), "Save", this);

QString setlistBtnStyle = "QPushButton { background-color: #444444; color: white; "
                          "padding: 4px 8px; font-size: 9pt; }";
m_btnAddSong->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle);
m_btnRemoveSong->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle);
m_btnSaveSetlist->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle);

slLayout->addWidget(m_btnAddSong);
slLayout->addWidget(m_btnRemoveSong);
slLayout->addWidget(m_btnSaveSetlist);

connect(m_btnAddSong,     &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleAddSongToSetlist);
connect(m_btnRemoveSong,  &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleRemoveSongFromSetlist);
connect(m_btnSaveSetlist, &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleSaveSetlist);

m_setlistActionsWidget->hide();
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_setlistActionsWidget);

m_settingsToolBar->addSeparator();   // visual divider before the main button row
// ... then continues with your existing container setup

Step 3 — Simplify onHamburgerClicked()

cpp
void MainWindow::onHamburgerClicked() {
    if (m_setlistView->isHidden()) {
        m_setlistView->show();
        mainSplitter->setSizes({250, this->width() - 250});
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("× Close Setlist");
        m_setlistActionsWidget->show();
    } else {
        m_setlistView->hide();
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("≡ Setlist");
        m_setlistActionsWidget->hide();
    }
}

Step 4 — Simplify handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked()

cpp
void MainWindow::handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked(const QModelIndex &index) {
    if (!index.parent().isValid()) return;

    if (!m_setlistView->isHidden()) {
        m_setlistView->hide();
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("≡ Setlist");
        m_setlistActionsWidget->hide();   // ← one clean line replaces all the old remove logic
    }

    setAppState(PlayAlong);
    this->setFocus();
}

Step 5 — Clean up handleSaveSetlist()

This one currently uses m_actSaveSetlist indirectly but is actually self-contained, so no changes needed there.

That's the full picture. The key insight is that nothing in the toolbar layout ever changes at runtime — the setlist zone is always there, it just becomes visible or invisible. Much more predictable behaviour and no ordering surprises. Good luck! 🎶

You said: Thanks, approeciate your help :-) I'm still not seeing the setlist buttons, have I hidden them away?

Thanks, approeciate your help :-) I'm still not seeing the setlist buttons, have I hidden them away?

2:26 pm

Claude responded: Looking at your code, the logic is all correct — the issue is subtle.

Looking at your code, the logic is all correct — the issue is subtle. In onHamburgerClicked() you're checking m_setlistView->isHidden() but in setAppState(PlayAlong) you hide mainSplitter entirely, which also hides m_setlistView as a side effect. So the hidden state of m_setlistView can get out of sync with whether the setlist panel is "open".

But the more immediate issue I can spot is this — let me trace the startup sequence:

  1. Constructor calls setupToolBar() — m_setlistActionsWidget is created and hidden ✓
  2. showEvent fires after 50ms and calls setAppState(Idle) or setAppState(OpenEdit)
  3. Neither state touches m_setlistActionsWidget — still hidden ✓
  4. User clicks hamburger → onHamburgerClicked() → m_setlistActionsWidget->show() ✓

That should work... so can you add a quick debug line to confirm the show is actually being reached?

cpp
void MainWindow::onHamburgerClicked() {
    if (m_setlistView->isHidden()) {
        m_setlistView->show();
        mainSplitter->setSizes({250, this->width() - 250});
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("× Close Setlist");
        m_setlistActionsWidget->show();
        qDebug() << ">>> Hamburger OPEN: setlistActionsWidget visible ="
                 << m_setlistActionsWidget->isVisible()
                 << "| parent visible =" << m_setlistActionsWidget->parentWidget()->isVisible();
    } else {
        m_setlistView->hide();
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("≡ Setlist");
        m_setlistActionsWidget->hide();
        qDebug() << ">>> Hamburger CLOSE";
    }
}

Also double-check your .h file has the new members declared and the old QAction* ones are removed — if m_setlistActionsWidget is still declared as a QAction* or not declared at all, Qt Creator can sometimes silently compile against the wrong thing. Can you paste the relevant section of your current mainwindow.h?

You said: ok, thanks.

ok, thanks...

2:29 pm

Claude responded: The header looks correct — all three QPushButton members are there, m_setlistActionsWidget is declared, and the old QAction members are gone.

The header looks correct — all three QPushButton* members are there, m_setlistActionsWidget is declared, and the old QAction* members are gone. That's all good.

There's one thing that jumps out though — m_settingsToolBar and m_setlistActionsWidget are declared in the public section at the top, while all the other UI members are in private. That's not causing the bug but worth tidying.

The real issue is likely this — can you check what your debug output shows after clicking the hamburger button? Specifically look for the line:

>>> Hamburger OPEN: setlistActionsWidget visible = ...

If you're not seeing that line at all, the button click isn't reaching onHamburgerClicked. If you see it but visible = false, then something is hiding the widget after show() is called — possibly the stylesheet being reapplied.

Also worth checking — did you add the debug line I suggested? And one quick sanity check, can you temporarily force the widget visible at the very end of setupToolBar() just to confirm it renders correctly at all:

cpp
    m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(container);

    // TEMPORARY TEST - remove after confirming
    m_setlistActionsWidget->show();
}

If the three buttons appear on startup with that line, the widget itself is fine and the problem is purely in the show/hide toggling logic. That would narrow it down quickly. 🎸

2:31 pm

Claude responded: Great news — the logs tell a very clear story!

Great news — the logs tell a very clear story! Look at this:

>>> Hamburger OPEN: setlistActionsWidget visible = true | parent visible = true
>>> Hamburger OPEN: setlistActionsWidget visible = true | parent visible = true

The hamburger is firing twice on startup (from the saved session restoring the last state), and visible = true confirms the widget is showing correctly. So the buttons are actually working! 🎉

The reason you're not seeing them is almost certainly the stylesheet override. Your Adaptic(default).qss is being applied globally via qApp->setStyleSheet(), and it's likely setting a background/text colour on QWidget or QPushButton that makes the buttons invisible against the toolbar background — or the QSS has rules that accidentally hide them.

A quick test — add an explicit override to your setlist button style to punch through the global QSS:

cpp
QString setlistBtnStyle = "QPushButton { background-color: #AA4444 !important; "
                          "color: white !important; "
                          "padding: 4px 8px; font-size: 9pt; "
                          "border: 2px solid red; }";  // make them obvious!

If bright red bordered buttons suddenly appear next to the hamburger, you've confirmed it's purely a styling conflict. Then you can tune the colours to match your theme properly.

The double-firing on startup is a separate minor issue — it's because showEvent restores the last session which was apparently in a state where the setlist was open. Nothing harmful, but worth noting for later. 🎸

You said: No I clicked it twise :-)

No I clicked it twise :-)

2:32 pm

Claude responded: Ha!

Ha! Of course — that makes perfect sense! 😄

So the buttons are working correctly then. The widget is showing and hiding as expected. It's purely a visibility/styling issue where the buttons are there but blending into the toolbar background because of the global QSS.

Try the red border test to confirm:

cpp
QString setlistBtnStyle = "QPushButton { background-color: #AA4444; "
                          "color: white; "
                          "padding: 4px 8px; font-size: 9pt; "
                          "border: 2px solid red; }";

If they pop into view, then it's just a matter of finding colours that work against your toolbar. Looking at your other toolbar buttons for reference — the hamburger itself uses #333333 background with white text, and your main container buttons use #004060 and #555555. So something like this should blend in naturally:

cpp
QString setlistBtnStyle = "QPushButton { background-color: #4A4A4A; "
                          "color: #FFFFFF; "
                          "padding: 4px 8px; font-size: 9pt; "
                          "border: 1px solid #666666; "
                          "border-radius: 3px; }";

The root cause is that qApp->setStyleSheet() with a global QSS can override widget-level styles unless they're specific enough. If the red border test works, that confirms it and you're just tuning colours from there. 🎸

You said: Full logs, several clicks!

Full logs, several clicks! still no sign of the buttons, could they be hidden some where else? Or, maybe we go back to 'standard' look, i.e. add smaller buttons, and enable/disable them assetlist is opened and closed?

2:42 pm

Claude responded: Looking at the logs more carefully — the widget is reporting visible = true but you can't see it.

Looking at the logs more carefully — the widget is reporting visible = true but you can't see it. The most likely culprit is that m_setlistActionsWidget has zero size because the QHBoxLayout inside it isn't being respected by the toolbar. This is a known Qt quirk — when you add a QWidget containing a layout to a QToolBar, the widget sometimes collapses to zero width if it doesn't have an explicit size hint.

Quick diagnostic — add this right after m_setlistActionsWidget->show() in onHamburgerClicked:

cpp
qDebug() << "setlistActionsWidget size =" << m_setlistActionsWidget->size();
qDebug() << "setlistActionsWidget geometry =" << m_setlistActionsWidget->geometry();

I'd bet it shows 0x0.

But given that, your instinct to go back to the enable/disable approach is actually the cleaner solution for a toolbar anyway. Here's how to do it cleanly — no show/hide, no sizing headaches:

In setupToolBar(), add the buttons directly to the toolbar (not wrapped in a container widget) and just disable them initially:

cpp
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_btnToggleSetlist);
m_settingsToolBar->addSeparator();

m_btnAddSong     = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/add_sg.png"), "Add", this);
m_btnRemoveSong  = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/remove_sg.png"), "Remove", this);
m_btnSaveSetlist = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/save_sl.png"), "Save", this);

QString setlistBtnStyle = "QPushButton { background-color: #4A4A4A; color: #FFFFFF; "
                          "padding: 4px 8px; font-size: 9pt; border: 1px solid #666; "
                          "border-radius: 3px; }"
                          "QPushButton:disabled { background-color: #2A2A2A; "
                          "color: #555555; border-color: #444; }";

m_btnAddSong->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle);
m_btnRemoveSong->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle);
m_btnSaveSetlist->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle);

m_btnAddSong->setEnabled(false);
m_btnRemoveSong->setEnabled(false);
m_btnSaveSetlist->setEnabled(false);

connect(m_btnAddSong,     &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleAddSongToSetlist);
connect(m_btnRemoveSong,  &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleRemoveSongFromSetlist);
connect(m_btnSaveSetlist, &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleSaveSetlist);

m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_btnAddSong);
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_btnRemoveSong);
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_btnSaveSetlist);

m_settingsToolBar->addSeparator();
// ... rest of toolbar (container etc.)

Then onHamburgerClicked just toggles enabled state:

cpp
void MainWindow::onHamburgerClicked() {
    if (m_setlistView->isHidden()) {
        m_setlistView->show();
        mainSplitter->setSizes({250, this->width() - 250});
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("× Close Setlist");
        m_btnAddSong->setEnabled(true);
        m_btnRemoveSong->setEnabled(true);
        m_btnSaveSetlist->setEnabled(true);
    } else {
        m_setlistView->hide();
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("≡ Setlist");
        m_btnAddSong->setEnabled(false);
        m_btnRemoveSong->setEnabled(false);
        m_btnSaveSetlist->setEnabled(false);
    }
}

And handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked similarly:

cpp
void MainWindow::handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked(const QModelIndex &index) {
    if (!index.parent().isValid()) return;

    if (!m_setlistView->isHidden()) {
        m_setlistView->hide();
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("≡ Setlist");
        m_btnAddSong->setEnabled(false);
        m_btnRemoveSong->setEnabled(false);
        m_btnSaveSetlist->setEnabled(false);
    }

    setAppState(PlayAlong);
    this->setFocus();
}

You can also remove m_setlistActionsWidget entirely from both the .h and .cpp — it's no longer needed. This approach is simpler, more predictable, and the disabled visual state gives the user a clear signal that those buttons only apply when the setlist is open. 🎸

2:42 pm

Claude responded: The logs confirm the enable/disable approach is working — the hamburger is firing correctly.

The logs confirm the enable/disable approach is working — the hamburger is firing correctly. But I notice you still have the old m_setlistActionsWidget debug line firing, which means you may still have the old show/hide code in place rather than the new enable/disable version.

Check your onHamburgerClicked — it should not reference m_setlistActionsWidget at all anymore. It should look exactly like this and nothing else:

cpp
void MainWindow::onHamburgerClicked() {
    if (m_setlistView->isHidden()) {
        m_setlistView->show();
        mainSplitter->setSizes({250, this->width() - 250});
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("× Close Setlist");
        m_btnAddSong->setEnabled(true);
        m_btnRemoveSong->setEnabled(true);
        m_btnSaveSetlist->setEnabled(true);
    } else {
        m_setlistView->hide();
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("≡ Setlist");
        m_btnAddSong->setEnabled(false);
        m_btnRemoveSong->setEnabled(false);
        m_btnSaveSetlist->setEnabled(false);
    }
}

And also remove m_setlistActionsWidget from your .h entirely — if it's still declared it may still be getting created in setupToolBar() and sitting invisibly between your hamburger and the new buttons, pushing them off screen or causing confusion.

Once those two things are clean, the three buttons should be sitting right next to the hamburger, dimmed when the setlist is closed and lit up when it's open. 🎸

You said: oh no, it is there, updated and just rebuilding now :-( sorry.

oh no, it *is there*, updated and just rebuilding now :-( sorry...

Claude responded: No need to apologise at all — this is exactly how development goes!

No need to apologise at all — this is exactly how development goes! Rebuilding and testing iteratively is the right process. 🙂

Take your time, let it build and run, and report back what you see. Fingers crossed the three buttons appear right next to the hamburger this time! 🎸

2:50 pm

Claude responded: Ah, the debug line is now reporting msetlistView->isVisible() not msetlistActionsWidget->isVisible() — so you updated the debug message but accidentally change…

Ah, the debug line is now reporting m_setlistView->isVisible() not m_setlistActionsWidget->isVisible() — so you updated the debug message but accidentally changed what it's actually checking! That's why it still says setlistActionsWidget visible = true in the text but is actually reporting the setlist view's visibility.

But more importantly — can you actually see the three buttons in the toolbar now? The logs look clean otherwise — no crashes, proper startup, audio loaded fine.

What does the toolbar look like visually? 🎸

You said: No :-( --> there's blue vertical line on the right hand side of the Close Setlist button, but no sign of changes when button is clicked.

No :-( --> there's blue vertical line on the right hand side of the Close Setlist button, but no sign of changes when button is clicked...

2:53 pm

Claude responded: Looking at the screenshot — ChordLab is looking really good by the way!

Looking at the screenshot — ChordLab is looking really good by the way! 🎉

I can see the issue clearly now. The toolbar shows × Close Setlist then immediately Open | Save | As... | Parse — those are your Fn buttons in Edit mode, not the setlist buttons. The three setlist buttons (Add/Remove/Save) are either zero-width or being swallowed somewhere.

That blue vertical line you see is almost certainly the addSeparator() between the setlist zone and the container — which means the three buttons exist in the layout but have collapsed to zero width, likely because the global QSS from Adaptic(default).qss is overriding your button styles and setting them to zero size or making them transparent.

Try this nuclear test — give each button a fixed width to force them to take up space regardless of QSS:

cpp
m_btnAddSong->setFixedWidth(70);
m_btnRemoveSong->setFixedWidth(70);
m_btnSaveSetlist->setFixedWidth(70);

Add those three lines right after setting their stylesheets in setupToolBar(). If three 70px-wide buttons suddenly appear next to the Close Setlist button, the QSS is definitely collapsing them and you just need to make the style more specific to win the override battle. 🎸

You said: // --- Setlist companion buttons (hidden until hamburger opens) ---

// --- Setlist companion buttons (hidden until hamburger opens) --- m_setlistActionsWidget = new QWidget(this); QHBoxLayout *slLayout = new QHBoxLayout(m_setlistActionsWidget); slLayout->setContentsMargins(2, 0, 2, 0); slLayout->setSpacing(4); m_btnAddSong = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/add_sg.png"), "Add", this); m_btnRemoveSong = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/remove_sg.png"), "Remove", this); m_btnSaveSetlist = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/save_sl.png"), "Save", this); QString setlistBtnStyle = "QPushButton { background-color: #AA4444 !important; " "color: white !important; " "padding: 4px 8px; font-size: 9pt; " "border: 2px solid red; }"; // make them obvious! // QString setlistBtnStyle = "QPushButton { background-color: #444444; color: white; " // "padding: 4px 8px; font-size: 9pt; }"; m_btnAddSong->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle); m_btnRemoveSong->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle); m_btnSaveSetlist->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle); m_btnAddSong->setFixedWidth(70); // style, size & location checking... m_btnRemoveSong->setFixedWidth(70); m_btnSaveSetlist->setFixedWidth(70); slLayout->addWidget(m_btnAddSong); slLayout->addWidget(m_btnRemoveSong); slLayout->addWidget(m_btnSaveSetlist); connect(m_btnAddSong, &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleAddSongToSetlist); connect(m_btnRemoveSong, &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleRemoveSongFromSetlist); connect(m_btnSaveSetlist, &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleSaveSetlist); m_setlistActionsWidget->hide(); m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_setlistActionsWidget); m_settingsToolBar->addSeparator(); m_btnTransposeUp = new QPushButton("Key Up"); --> maybe need a bit of a clean up see if we have the setlist buttons in the right place yet> Thanks...

3:12 pm

Claude responded: I can see the problem!

I can see the problem! You're still using the old show/hide approach with m_setlistActionsWidget — but we decided to abandon that and go with enable/disable with buttons added directly to the toolbar. The two approaches are mixed together here.

The fix is to remove the m_setlistActionsWidget wrapper entirely and add the buttons directly to the toolbar:

cpp
// --- Setlist companion buttons (enable/disable approach) ---
m_btnAddSong     = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/add_sg.png"), "Add", this);
m_btnRemoveSong  = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/remove_sg.png"), "Remove", this);
m_btnSaveSetlist = new QPushButton(QIcon(":/resources/icons/save_sl.png"), "Save", this);

QString setlistBtnStyle = "QPushButton { background-color: #4A4A4A; color: #FFFFFF; "
                          "padding: 4px 8px; font-size: 9pt; border: 1px solid #666; "
                          "border-radius: 3px; }"
                          "QPushButton:disabled { background-color: #2A2A2A; "
                          "color: #555555; border-color: #444; }";

m_btnAddSong->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle);
m_btnRemoveSong->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle);
m_btnSaveSetlist->setStyleSheet(setlistBtnStyle);

m_btnAddSong->setEnabled(false);
m_btnRemoveSong->setEnabled(false);
m_btnSaveSetlist->setEnabled(false);

connect(m_btnAddSong,     &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleAddSongToSetlist);
connect(m_btnRemoveSong,  &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleRemoveSongFromSetlist);
connect(m_btnSaveSetlist, &QPushButton::clicked, this, &MainWindow::handleSaveSetlist);

// Add DIRECTLY to toolbar — no wrapper widget!
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_btnAddSong);
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_btnRemoveSong);
m_settingsToolBar->addWidget(m_btnSaveSetlist);

m_settingsToolBar->addSeparator();
// ... continue with m_btnTransposeUp etc.

And you can remove m_setlistActionsWidget from your .h too — it's no longer needed at all. 🎸

You said: Hi Cluadu, well spotted - this led me to find another clean candidate --> void MainWindow::handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked(const QModelIndex &index) {

Hi Cluadu, well spotted - this led me to find another clean candidate --> void MainWindow::handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked(const QModelIndex &index) { if (!index.parent().isValid()) return; if (!m_setlistView->isHidden()) { m_setlistView->hide(); m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("≡ Setlist"); // m_setlistActionsWidget->hide(); // ← one clean line replaces all the old remove logic } setAppState(PlayAlong); this->setFocus(); } --> what do you suggest?? Many thanks..

3:18 pm

Claude responded: Easy fix — just replace the commented-out line with the three setEnabled(false) calls to match the new approach:

Easy fix — just replace the commented-out line with the three setEnabled(false) calls to match the new approach:

cpp
void MainWindow::handleSetlistItemDoubleClicked(const QModelIndex &index) {
    if (!index.parent().isValid()) return;

    if (!m_setlistView->isHidden()) {
        m_setlistView->hide();
        m_btnToggleSetlist->setText("≡ Setlist");
        m_btnAddSong->setEnabled(false);
        m_btnRemoveSong->setEnabled(false);
        m_btnSaveSetlist->setEnabled(false);
    }

    setAppState(PlayAlong);
    this->setFocus();
}

That keeps it perfectly consistent with onHamburgerClicked() — both code paths now use the same enable/disable pattern. Nice and symmetrical. 🎸

You said: perfect :-)

perfect :-)

3:18 pm

Claude responded: Excellent!

Excellent! 🎉

So to recap what ended up being a good journey — you now have:

  • ✅ Setlist Add/Remove/Save buttons sitting right next to the hamburger where they belong
  • ✅ They dim when the setlist is closed and light up when it's open
  • ✅ Double-clicking a song closes the setlist panel and dims the buttons correctly
  • ✅ No more fragile add/remove toolbar thrashing — just clean enable/disable

The enable/disable approach turned out to be more robust than the show/hide wrapper idea, and arguably better UX too since the user can always see the buttons are there — they just know they're inactive when the setlist is closed.

What's next for ChordLab? 🎸🎶

You said: no.

no... it was a great journey, many thanks... Now, I reckon we have the basis of a very handy app. 🙏🚀👍... The next improvement is a core function - can we please look at adding better 'granularity' in the zooming mechanism. A User can use the Ctrl+'-' and '+' keys, but we have a very clunky prototyping zoom function. Improvements could be maybe a) retain the coarse zoom and add a fine grained control which zooms in (much) smaller increments. This is to get a piece to show with the largest font possible so lyrics and chords are visible even in "live" circumstances... b. maybe a separate column +/- control to experiment pushing sections around to get the best fit... Can you see a way to start refining the zoom-factors? Many thanks...

4:23 pm

Claude responded: Great ambitions — this is exactly the kind of refinement that makes an app genuinely usable on stage!

Great ambitions — this is exactly the kind of refinement that makes an app genuinely usable on stage! Let me analyse what you currently have and then propose a clean upgrade path.

What You Have Now

cpp
int baseFontSize = 10 + (m_zoomScaleLevel * 2);  // steps of 2pt

m_zoomScaleLevel runs from -4 to +6, giving font sizes from 2pt to 22pt in 2pt jumps. And columns are calculated automatically by the Radar engine based on font size and widget width. The two are tightly coupled — zooming in also reduces columns.

Proposed Upgrade — Two Independent Controls

a) Fine Zoom — fractional font scaling

Split m_zoomScaleLevel into two independent members:

cpp
int m_zoomCoarse = 0;    // existing Ctrl+/-  steps of 2pt (-4 to +6)
int m_zoomFine   = 0;    // new              steps of 0.5pt (-4 to +4)

Then in updatePlayAlongLayoutDensity():

cpp
double baseFontSize = 10.0 + (m_zoomCoarse * 2.0) + (m_zoomFine * 0.5);

For the fine control, add two small buttons to the toolbar or handle new key combos:

cpp
// Ctrl+Shift+Plus / Minus for fine zoom
if (event->modifiers() & (Qt::ControlModifier | Qt::ShiftModifier)) {
    if (event->key() == Qt::Key_Plus || event->key() == Qt::Key_Equal) {
        if (m_zoomFine < 8) m_zoomFine++;
        updatePlayAlongLayoutDensity();
        event->accept(); return;
    }
    if (event->key() == Qt::Key_Minus) {
        if (m_zoomFine > -8) m_zoomFine--;
        updatePlayAlongLayoutDensity();
        event->accept(); return;
    }
}

b) Manual Column Override — decouple columns from zoom

Add a separate column override member:

cpp
int m_columnOverride = 0;   // 0 = auto (Radar decides), 1-4 = manual lock

Then in parseChordProToGrid() where columns are calculated:

cpp
int numCols = (m_columnOverride > 0) ? m_columnOverride 
                                      : m_currentSongMetrics.targetColumns;

Key bindings for column control — something like Ctrl+Left/Right:

cpp
if (event->modifiers() & Qt::ControlModifier) {
    if (event->key() == Qt::Key_Left) {
        if (m_columnOverride > 1) m_columnOverride--;
        else m_columnOverride = 0; // back to auto
        parseChordProToGrid(m_rawSongContent);
        event->accept(); return;
    }
    if (event->key() == Qt::Key_Right) {
        int current = (m_columnOverride > 0) ? m_columnOverride 
                                              : m_currentSongMetrics.targetColumns;
        if (current < 4) m_columnOverride = current + 1;
        parseChordProToGrid(m_rawSongContent);
        event->accept(); return;
    }
}

Status Bar Feedback

Both controls benefit from live feedback so the musician knows what they've dialled in:

cpp
statusBar()->showMessage(
    QString("Font: %1pt | Columns: %2%3")
        .arg(baseFontSize, 0, 'f', 1)
        .arg(numCols)
        .arg(m_columnOverride > 0 ? " (manual)" : " (auto)")
);

Summary of Changes Needed

WhatWhereNew member
Split zoom into coarse+finekeyPressEvent, updatePlayAlongLayoutDensitym_zoomFine
Column overridekeyPressEvent, parseChordProToGridm_columnOverride
Status bar feedbackboth functions—
Save/restore per songsaveSongLayoutPreference / loadSongLayoutPreferenceboth new members

Want me to write out the full updated versions of the affected functions? 🎸

You said: Yes, please but I can provide a little more food for thought first :-).

Yes, please but I can provide a little more food for thought first :-)... there is an existing cross platform app called SongBookPro (which I own multiple licences for - but is not able to provide adequate features - for [myself] jamming, teaching and gigs). It does handle zoom like a million dollars though... it is only lacking in audio play-along and teaching features. So a future feature for ChordLab to add an image/sheet music fragment .png/.jpg would be good down the track... But, for now you can see the high quality SBPro screen shot👍is very good - so getting closer to that is a good short term objective!

4:31 pm

Claude responded: What a great reference point!

What a great reference point! Looking at both screenshots, the SongBookPro layout is clearly the target to aim for. A few observations:

What SBPro does well that ChordLab can learn from:

  • Compact but readable font — looks around 11-12pt with tight line spacing
  • Chords and lyrics are visually well separated but not wasteful of space
  • 3-column layout fits the whole song on one screen — no scrolling needed on stage!
  • Section headers (Intro, Verse 1 etc.) are bold but compact
  • The chord/lyric spacing alignment is very clean

ChordLab's current render (image 2) looks promising — the 2-column layout with Yesterday is actually quite good already, the fonts just need tightening up.

So before I write the full code, let me propose the refined zoom architecture with your goals in mind:

Proposed Control Scheme

ControlActionStep Size
Ctrl +/-Coarse font zoom2pt
Ctrl+Shift +/-Fine font zoom0.5pt
Ctrl+Right/LeftColumn +/- override1 column
Ctrl+0Reset all to auto—

And the key insight from SBPro is that line height and section spacing matter as much as font size. So the fine zoom should scale those too:

cpp
// Current - too coarse
int baseFontSize = 10 + (m_zoomCoarse * 2);

// Proposed - fine grained
double baseFontSize    = 10.0 + (m_zoomCoarse * 2.0) + (m_zoomFine * 0.5);
double lineHeight      = 1.15 + (m_zoomCoarse * 0.04) + (m_zoomFine * 0.01);
int sectionSpacing     = 12   + (m_zoomCoarse * 3)    + (m_zoomFine);
int headerSpacing      = 4    + (m_zoomCoarse)         + (m_zoomFine / 2);

This means a fine zoom nudge tightens or loosens everything proportionally — exactly like SBPro feels.

Shall I write out the full implementation across all four affected areas:

  1. mainwindow.h — new members m_zoomFine, m_columnOverride
  2. keyPressEvent — new key bindings
  3. updatePlayAlongLayoutDensity() — refined scaling formula
  4. saveSongLayoutPreference / loadSongLayoutPreference — persist the new values

...and also the status bar feedback so you can see exactly what's dialled in? 🎸

You said: Great insights - 🙏🎶 - and I suspect there is a bedrock of issues to resolve - but I'm looking at the SBPro fonts in comparison to monospaced (easy but restri…

Great insights - 🙏🎶 - and I suspect there is a bedrock of issues to resolve - but I'm looking at the SBPro fonts in comparison to monospaced (easy but restricted) approach in my layouts...

4:34 pm

Claude responded: Great observation!

Great observation! This is actually a fundamental architectural decision. Let me break down what SBPro is doing vs what ChordLab currently does:

The Core Font Problem

ChordLab currently uses Consolas monospaced for both chords and lyrics. This is easy to implement because every character is the same width, making chord-over-lyric alignment trivial — you just count characters.

SBPro is clearly using a proportional font (looks like a sans-serif, possibly Segoe UI or similar) for lyrics, while chords appear to be in a slightly different weight. This looks far more natural and readable at a glance on stage.

The challenge is that proportional fonts break the simple character-counting alignment approach that ChordLab currently uses in parseChordProToGrid(). This section of your code:

cpp
// Dual-Cursor Trackers tracking absolute character coordinates
int visualChordCursor = 0;
int visualLyricCursor = 0;

...fundamentally assumes monospaced. With proportional fonts, an i and a W take completely different widths, so character counting no longer maps to pixel positions.

Three Possible Approaches

Option A — Keep Monospaced, Improve the Font Choice

Easiest path. Swap Consolas for a more readable monospaced font:

  • Courier Prime — designed for readability, feels less "code-like"
  • Roboto Mono — modern, clean, very legible
  • Inconsolata — compact and musical-feeling

This gets you 80% of the way to SBPro with minimal code changes.

Option B — Proportional Lyrics, Monospaced Chords

A hybrid approach used by some professional chart apps:

  • Lyrics in a proportional font like Segoe UI or Arial
  • Chords in a monospaced or fixed font positioned above using HTML <table> cells per beat

This requires restructuring parseChordProToGrid() to think in beat cells rather than character positions. Each beat gets a table cell, chord goes in the top row, lyric syllable in the bottom — the table handles alignment automatically.

html
<table><tr>
  <td>Em</td><td>A7</td><td>Dm</td><td>Bb</td>
</tr><tr>
  <td>Yes-ter-</td><td>day,</td><td>all my</td><td>trou-bles</td>
</tr></table>

Option C — Full Pixel-Perfect Proportional Rendering

The "right" solution long term — use QFontMetrics to measure actual pixel widths of each text fragment and position chords using absolute pixel offsets via margin-left in the HTML. This is how proper chart renderers work but it's significantly more complex.

My Recommendation for ChordLab Right Now

Start with Option A — it's a one-line change that will immediately make the app feel more polished, and it doesn't break anything. Then plan Option B as the next architectural step, because the table-cell approach is actually cleaner than the current cursor-tracking code and opens the door to proportional fonts naturally.

The zoom refinement we discussed still applies regardless of which font approach you choose — it's orthogonal to the font question.

Want me to write out Option A as an immediate improvement, and sketch the Option B table-cell architecture so you can see what that migration would look like? 🎸

You said: yep, I agree.

yep, I agree. A later version might contemplate proportional fonts - and I'm happy to go with Option A in this "freebie for my students and friends and people I don't know" version...👍

4:37 pm

Claude responded: Love that spirit — a freebie for students, friends and strangers is a noble goal!

Love that spirit — a freebie for students, friends and strangers is a noble goal! 🎓🎸

Option A is the right call for now. Here's the complete picture:

Immediate Font Improvements

First, bundle a better monospaced font as a resource. Courier Prime is the best choice for music charts — it was literally designed as a readable alternative to Courier, has a slightly warmer feel, and is free/open source (SIL licence, safe to bundle).

Download from: https://fonts.google.com/specimen/Courier+Prime

Add to your resources.qrc:

xml
<file>resources/fonts/CourierPrime-Regular.ttf</file>
<file>resources/fonts/CourierPrime-Bold.ttf</file>

Register in main.cpp before MainWindow is created:

cpp
QFontDatabase::addApplicationFont(":/resources/fonts/CourierPrime-Regular.ttf");
QFontDatabase::addApplicationFont(":/resources/fonts/CourierPrime-Bold.ttf");

Then in setupLayout() swap your editor font:

cpp
QFont monoFont("Courier Prime", 10);
// fallback chain if font isn't loaded:
monoFont.setStyleHint(QFont::TypeWriter);
originalEditor->setFont(monoFont);
parsedEditor->setFont(monoFont);

Refined CSS in updatePlayAlongLayoutDensity()

This is where the biggest visual improvement comes from — tightening up the spacing to be closer to SBPro:

cpp
void MainWindow::updatePlayAlongLayoutDensity() {
    double baseFontSize    = 10.0 + (m_zoomCoarse * 2.0) + (m_zoomFine * 0.5);
    double lineHeight      = 1.10 + (m_zoomCoarse * 0.03) + (m_zoomFine * 0.008);
    int sectionSpacing     = 8    + (m_zoomCoarse * 2)    + m_zoomFine;
    int headerSpacing      = 3    + (m_zoomCoarse / 2);
    int chordFontSize      = baseFontSize;        // same size, different weight
    int sectionHeaderSize  = baseFontSize - 0.5;  // slightly smaller than lyrics

    QString bgColor    = (m_currentTheme == Dark) ? "#20204f" : "#e8f0f8";
    QString txtColor   = (m_currentTheme == Dark) ? "#E0E0E0" : "#1a1a1a";
    QString chordColor = (m_currentTheme == Dark) ? "#ffb060" : "#aa1111";
    QString headColor  = (m_currentTheme == Dark) ? "#6699ff" : "#0055aa";

    // ... metadata extraction (keep as-is) ...

    QString baseHtml = "<html><head><style>"
        "body {"
        "  background-color: " + bgColor + ";"
        "  color: " + txtColor + ";"
        "  margin: 12px 18px;"
        "  padding: 0;"
        "  font-family: 'Courier Prime', 'Courier New', monospace;"
        "}"
        ".section-heading {"
        "  font-size: " + QString::number(sectionHeaderSize, 'f', 1) + "pt;"
        "  color: " + headColor + ";"
        "  font-weight: bold;"
        "  font-family: 'Courier Prime', 'Courier New', monospace;"
        "  margin: 0 0 " + QString::number(headerSpacing) + "px 0;"
        "  padding: 0;"
        "  border-bottom: 1px solid " + headColor + "33;"  // subtle underline
        "}"
        ".song-section {"
        "  margin-bottom: " + QString::number(sectionSpacing) + "px;"
        "}"
        ".chord-line {"
        "  font-weight: bold;"
        "  color: " + chordColor + ";"
        "  font-family: 'Courier Prime', 'Courier New', monospace;"
        "  font-size: " + QString::number(chordFontSize, 'f', 1) + "pt;"
        "  margin: 0; padding: 0;"
        "  line-height: " + QString::number(lineHeight * 0.95, 'f', 3) + ";"
        "}"
        ".lyric-text {"
        "  color: " + txtColor + ";"
        "  font-family: 'Courier Prime', 'Courier New', monospace;"
        "  font-size: " + QString::number(baseFontSize, 'f', 1) + "pt;"
        "  margin: 0 0 2px 0; padding: 0;"
        "  line-height: " + QString::number(lineHeight, 'f', 3) + ";"
        "}"
        "</style></head><body>"
        "<div style='line-height: " + QString::number(lineHeight, 'f', 3) + ";'>"
        + headerHtml + m_parsedSongContentGrid +
        "</div></body></html>";

    parsedEditor->setHtml(baseHtml);

    // Live status feedback
    statusBar()->showMessage(
        QString("Font: %1pt | Columns: %2%3 | Zoom: %4 coarse / %5 fine")
            .arg(baseFontSize, 0, 'f', 1)
            .arg(m_columnOverride > 0 ? m_columnOverride : m_currentSongMetrics.targetColumns)
            .arg(m_columnOverride > 0 ? " (manual)" : " (auto)")
            .arg(m_zoomCoarse)
            .arg(m_zoomFine),
        4000
    );
}

New Members in mainwindow.h

cpp
// Replace m_zoomScaleLevel with these two:
int m_zoomCoarse = 0;    // Ctrl+/-    steps of 2pt
int m_zoomFine   = 0;    // Ctrl+Shift+/-  steps of 0.5pt
int m_columnOverride = 0; // 0=auto, 1-4=manual lock

And anywhere m_zoomScaleLevel is currently used, replace with m_zoomCoarse.

Updated keyPressEvent

cpp
void MainWindow::keyPressEvent(QKeyEvent *event) {
    if (currentState == PlayAlong) {
        bool ctrl  = event->modifiers() & Qt::ControlModifier;
        bool shift = event->modifiers() & Qt::ShiftModifier;
        int key    = event->key();

        if (ctrl && !shift) {
            if (key == Qt::Key_Plus || key == Qt::Key_Equal) {
                if (m_zoomCoarse < 6) m_zoomCoarse++;
                saveSongLayoutPreference(m_currentFilePath);
                parseChordProToGrid(m_rawSongContent);
                event->accept(); return;
            }
            if (key == Qt::Key_Minus) {
                if (m_zoomCoarse > -4) m_zoomCoarse--;
                saveSongLayoutPreference(m_currentFilePath);
                parseChordProToGrid(m_rawSongContent);
                event->accept(); return;
            }
            if (key == Qt::Key_0) {
                m_zoomCoarse = 0;
                m_zoomFine = 0;
                m_columnOverride = 0;
                saveSongLayoutPreference(m_currentFilePath);
                parseChordProToGrid(m_rawSongContent);
                event->accept(); return;
            }
            if (key == Qt::Key_Right) {
                int current = (m_columnOverride > 0) ? m_columnOverride
                                                      : m_currentSongMetrics.targetColumns;
                if (current < 4) m_columnOverride = current + 1;
                parseChordProToGrid(m_rawSongContent);
                event->accept(); return;
            }
            if (key == Qt::Key_Left) {
                if (m_columnOverride > 1) m_columnOverride--;
                else m_columnOverride = 0;  // back to auto
                parseChordProToGrid(m_rawSongContent);
                event->accept(); return;
            }
        }

        if (ctrl && shift) {
            if (key == Qt::Key_Plus || key == Qt::Key_Equal) {
                if (m_zoomFine < 8) m_zoomFine++;
                saveSongLayoutPreference(m_currentFilePath);
                updatePlayAlongLayoutDensity();  // no need to re-parse, just re-render
                event->accept(); return;
            }
            if (key == Qt::Key_Minus) {
                if (m_zoomFine > -8) m_zoomFine--;
                saveSongLayoutPreference(m_currentFilePath);
                updatePlayAlongLayoutDensity();
                event->accept(); return;
            }
        }

        // Spacebar playback toggle (keep as-is)
        if (key == Qt::Key_Space) {
            if (m_mediaPlayer->playbackState() == QMediaPlayer::PlayingState) {
                m_mediaPlayer->pause();
            } else {
                if (!m_selectedAudioPath.isEmpty()) m_mediaPlayer->play();
            }
            event->accept(); return;
        }
    }
    QMainWindow::keyPressEvent(event);
}

Updated Save/Load Layout Preference

cpp
void MainWindow::saveSongLayoutPreference(const QString &filePath) {
    if (filePath.isEmpty()) return;
    QSettings settings;
    QString songKey = QFileInfo(filePath).fileName();
    settings.beginGroup("SongLayouts/" + songKey);
    settings.setValue("hasOverride", true);
    settings.setValue("zoomCoarse", m_zoomCoarse);
    settings.setValue("zoomFine", m_zoomFine);
    settings.setValue("columnOverride", m_columnOverride);
    settings.endGroup();
}

void MainWindow::loadSongLayoutPreference(const QString &filePath) {
    if (filePath.isEmpty()) return;
    QSettings settings;
    QString songKey = QFileInfo(filePath).fileName();
    settings.beginGroup("SongLayouts/" + songKey);
    if (settings.value("hasOverride", false).toBool()) {
        m_zoomCoarse      = settings.value("zoomCoarse", 0).toInt();
        m_zoomFine        = settings.value("zoomFine", 0).toInt();
        m_columnOverride  = settings.value("columnOverride", 0).toInt();
    } else {
        m_zoomCoarse     = 0;
        m_zoomFine       = 0;
        m_columnOverride = 0;
    }
    settings.endGroup();
}

That's the complete set of changes. The key things that will make ChordLab feel much closer to SBPro visually are the Courier Prime font and the tighter margin: 0; padding: 0 discipline in the CSS — SBPro has very little wasted vertical space, and that's mostly a CSS tightening exercise. 🎸

You said: Wow, I'll get started.

Wow, I'll get started... maybe I can check with you upfront if this approach works across macOS/Linux???

4:41 pm

Claude responded: Great question to ask upfront!

Great question to ask upfront! Let me go through each element:

Font (Courier Prime) — ✅ Safe on all platforms

Bundling the .ttf files as Qt resources and registering with QFontDatabase::addApplicationFont() is completely cross-platform. Qt handles font loading identically on Windows, macOS and Linux. The fallback chain also protects you:

cpp
"font-family: 'Courier Prime', 'Courier New', monospace;"
  • Windows → Courier Prime (bundled) ✅
  • macOS → Courier Prime (bundled) ✅
  • Linux → Courier Prime (bundled) ✅

Even if the resource loading failed for any reason, Courier New is present on Windows/macOS and monospace maps to whatever the system default is on Linux.

QSettings — ⚠️ Minor awareness needed

QSettings::IniFormat which you're already using is the safest cross-platform choice. The file just lands in different locations:

  • Windows → %APPDATA%\AldoMusic\ChordLab.ini
  • macOS → ~/Library/Preferences/AldoMusic/ChordLab.ini
  • Linux → ~/.config/AldoMusic/ChordLab.ini

No code changes needed — Qt handles this automatically. ✅

File Paths — ⚠️ One issue to watch

This pattern you're using is fine on Windows but will need attention for macOS/Linux:

cpp
QString fullPath = QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath() 
                   + "/resources/pieces/" + relativePath;

On macOS, when built as a .app bundle, applicationDirPath() points inside the bundle (ChordLab.app/Contents/MacOS/), so your resources folder would need to be at ChordLab.app/Contents/MacOS/resources/ which is non-standard. The proper macOS approach uses:

cpp
// For bundled resources (read-only):
QStandardPaths::writableLocation(QStandardPaths::AppDataLocation)

// Or for resources shipped with the app:
QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath() + "/../Resources/pieces/"  // macOS bundle path

A clean cross-platform helper would be:

cpp
QString MainWindow::getResourcesPath() {
#ifdef Q_OS_MACOS
    // Inside .app bundle: Contents/Resources/
    return QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath() + "/../Resources";
#else
    // Windows and Linux: next to the executable
    return QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath() + "/resources";
#endif
}

Then use getResourcesPath() + "/pieces/" + relativePath everywhere. This is probably the biggest cross-platform issue in the current codebase and worth sorting before the macOS/Linux port.

HTML/CSS Rendering — ✅ Safe

Qt's QTextEdit HTML renderer is the same engine across all platforms — your CSS will render identically. The only caveat is that Qt's HTML subset doesn't support everything a browser does, but everything you're using (font-size, color, margin, padding, line-height) is all well within Qt's supported subset. ✅

Keyboard Shortcuts — ✅ Safe

Qt::ControlModifier correctly maps to:

  • Windows/Linux → Ctrl
  • macOS → Cmd (which is what Mac users expect)

So your Ctrl+/- zoom shortcuts will feel native on all three platforms. ✅

Qt Multimedia (FFmpeg) — ✅ Mostly safe

You're already using the FFmpeg backend which Qt6 bundles. This works on all three platforms as long as you deploy the FFmpeg libraries with the app. windeployqt, macdeployqt and linuxdeployqt all handle this automatically. ✅

Summary

ComponentStatusAction needed
Courier Prime font✅ SafeNone
QSettings IniFormat✅ SafeNone
File paths⚠️ macOS issueAdd getResourcesPath() helper
HTML/CSS rendering✅ SafeNone
Keyboard shortcuts✅ SafeNone
Qt Multimedia✅ SafeNone

The file path issue is the only meaningful cross-platform problem in the current codebase — worth adding that helper function sooner rather than later so it doesn't become a bigger migration headache later. Everything else in the zoom/font refactor will work cleanly across all three platforms. 🎸

You said: ok, I agree - let's make sure we are setting up for success rather than headaches/failure :-) Before going on let's keep this level of functionality, and resto…

ok, I agree - let's make sure we are setting up for success rather than headaches/failure :-) *Before* going on let's keep this level of functionality, and restore full 'Qt6 cross-platform-ness'. Can we pls go through and see how we go inserting the new getResourcesPath approach? Should or can I just search and replace any parts of the update? Thanks again...

4:51 pm

Claude responded: Great disciplined approach — sort the foundations before adding features!

Great disciplined approach — sort the foundations before adding features! 🎯

Let me identify every place in your codebase where paths are constructed that need updating. Based on the code I've seen, here are all the occurrences:

Step 1 — Add to mainwindow.h

In the private: section add:

cpp
QString getResourcesPath() const;

Step 2 — Add to mainwindow.cpp

Add this new function — put it near the top with the other simple helpers:

cpp
QString MainWindow::getResourcesPath() const {
#ifdef Q_OS_MACOS
    return QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath() + "/../Resources";
#else
    return QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath() + "/resources";
#endif
}

Step 3 — Search and Replace targets

Here are every specific instance to update, with exact before/after:

In handleSetlistItemClicked():

cpp
// BEFORE:
QString fullPath = QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath() + "/resources/pieces/" + relativePath;

// AFTER:
QString fullPath = getResourcesPath() + "/pieces/" + relativePath;

In handleAddSongToSetlist():

cpp
// BEFORE:
QDir piecesDir(QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath() + "/resources/pieces");

// AFTER:
QDir piecesDir(getResourcesPath() + "/pieces");

In handleSaveSetlist():

cpp
// BEFORE:
QString setlistPath = QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath() + "/resources/setlists/" + parentSetlist->text();

// AFTER:
QString setlistPath = getResourcesPath() + "/setlists/" + parentSetlist->text();

In onLoadSetlistTriggered():

cpp
// BEFORE:
QString setlistDir = QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath() + "/resources/setlists";

// AFTER:
QString setlistDir = getResourcesPath() + "/setlists";

In handleFileOpen():

cpp
// BEFORE:
QString appDir = QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath();
QString initialPath = QDir(appDir + "/resources/pieces").exists() ?
                          appDir + "/resources/pieces" : QDir::homePath();

// AFTER:
QString initialPath = QDir(getResourcesPath() + "/pieces").exists() ?
                          getResourcesPath() + "/pieces" : QDir::homePath();

In handleFileSave():

cpp
// BEFORE:
QString appDir = QCoreApplication::applicationDirPath();
QString initialPath = appDir + "/resources/pieces";
if (!QDir(initialPath).exists()) {
    initialPath = QDir::homePath();
}

// AFTER:
QString initialPath = getResourcesPath() + "/pieces";
if (!QDir(initialPath).exists()) {
    initialPath = QDir::homePath();
}

In checkForCompanionAudio():

This one is already using QFileInfo based on the passed-in chordProPath so it doesn't construct a resources path independently — no change needed there. ✅

Step 4 — Verify no others remain

Do a project-wide search in Qt Creator for these strings to make sure nothing is missed:

/resources/pieces
/resources/setlists
/resources/styles
/resources/fonts
/resources/icons
applicationDirPath() + "/resources"

Note that /resources/styles and /resources/icons are Qt resource system paths (prefixed with :/) so those are fine as-is — the :/ prefix means they're compiled into the binary and don't touch the filesystem. Only the paths without :/ need updating.

Quick Sanity Check

After making these changes, add a temporary debug line at the top of onLoadSetlistTriggered() to verify the path resolves correctly on your current Windows build before you ever touch a Mac:

cpp
qDebug() << "[Platform] Resources path:" << getResourcesPath();

On Windows you should see something like:

[Platform] Resources path: "D:/Qt/projects/ChordLabV002A/build/.../resources"

That confirms the #else branch is working correctly, and when you eventually build on macOS the #ifdef Q_OS_MACOS branch will kick in automatically. 🎸

You said: Maybe we leave font selection up to the User to pick from 4 candidates?

Maybe we leave font selection up to the User to pick from 4 candidates? The only restriction would be if spacing was an issue for multiple fonts *at-once* so we restrict selection to a single default font - but make it an option/preference? I like all - ut if any are not cross platform we omit them - and I'm fine to go with Courier Prime as the system default? 👍👍

5:07 pm

Claude responded: Great thinking — a font preference is a very professional touch and gives users ownership of their experience!

Great thinking — a font preference is a very professional touch and gives users ownership of their experience! 🎨

Looking at your four candidates:

Cross-Platform Verdict

FontTypeCross-PlatformNotes
Courier PrimeStatic✅ Bundle itWarmest, most "musical" feel
Libertinus MonoStatic✅ Bundle itElegant, serif mono — great for classical/jazz
Red Hat MonoVariable✅ Bundle itClean, modern, tech feel
Cascadia MonoVariable✅ Bundle itVery readable bold, Microsoft open source

All four are Google Fonts / open source with licences safe to bundle. Variable fonts (Red Hat Mono, Cascadia Mono) need a note though — Qt6's HTML renderer in QTextEdit has limited variable font support. They'll work but you may not get the full weight range. For QFont in the editor pane they'll work fine.

Implementation Plan

Step 1 — Bundle all four in resources.qrc

xml
<qresource prefix="/resources/fonts">
    <file>resources/fonts/CourierPrime-Regular.ttf</file>
    <file>resources/fonts/CourierPrime-Bold.ttf</file>
    <file>resources/fonts/LibertinusMono-Regular.otf</file>
    <file>resources/fonts/RedHatMono-Regular.ttf</file>
    <file>resources/fonts/RedHatMono-Bold.ttf</file>
    <file>resources/fonts/CascadiaMono-Regular.ttf</file>
    <file>resources/fonts/CascadiaMono-Bold.ttf</file>
</qresource>

Step 2 — Register all in main.cpp

cpp
int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    QApplication a(argc, argv);

    // Register all bundled fonts before MainWindow loads
    QFontDatabase::addApplicationFont(":/resources/fonts/CourierPrime-Regular.ttf");
    QFontDatabase::addApplicationFont(":/resources/fonts/CourierPrime-Bold.ttf");
    QFontDatabase::addApplicationFont(":/resources/fonts/LibertinusMono-Regular.otf");
    QFontDatabase::addApplicationFont(":/resources/fonts/RedHatMono-Regular.ttf");
    QFontDatabase::addApplicationFont(":/resources/fonts/RedHatMono-Bold.ttf");
    QFontDatabase::addApplicationFont(":/resources/fonts/CascadiaMono-Regular.ttf");
    QFontDatabase::addApplicationFont(":/resources/fonts/CascadiaMono-Bold.ttf");

    MainWindow w;
    w.show();
    return a.exec();
}

Step 3 — Add to mainwindow.h

cpp
// Font preference
QString m_currentFont = "Courier Prime";  // default

static const QStringList AVAILABLE_FONTS;  // defined in .cpp

Step 4 — Define in mainwindow.cpp

cpp
const QStringList MainWindow::AVAILABLE_FONTS = {
    "Courier Prime",
    "Libertinus Mono", 
    "Red Hat Mono",
    "Cascadia Mono"
};

Step 5 — Add to Preferences menu in setupMenus()

cpp
QMenu *fontMenu = prefMenu->addMenu("Display Font");
QActionGroup *fontGroup = new QActionGroup(this);
fontGroup->setExclusive(true);

QSettings settings;
QString savedFont = settings.value("display/font", "Courier Prime").toString();
m_currentFont = savedFont;

for (const QString &fontName : AVAILABLE_FONTS) {
    QAction *action = fontMenu->addAction(fontName);
    action->setCheckable(true);
    action->setActionGroup(fontGroup);
    if (fontName == savedFont) action->setChecked(true);

    connect(action, &QAction::triggered, this, [=]() {
        m_currentFont = fontName;
        QSettings s;
        s.setValue("display/font", fontName);

        // Update both editors immediately
        QFont f(fontName, 10);
        f.setStyleHint(QFont::TypeWriter);
        originalEditor->setFont(f);
        parsedEditor->setFont(f);

        // Re-render with new font
        if (currentState == PlayAlong) {
            parseChordProToGrid(m_rawSongContent);
        } else if (currentState == OpenEdit) {
            parsedEditor->setHtml(runInitialParse(m_rawSongContent));
        }

        statusBar()->showMessage("Font changed to: " + fontName, 3000);
    });
}

Step 6 — Use m_currentFont everywhere fonts are referenced

In setupLayout():

cpp
QFont monoFont(m_currentFont, 10);
monoFont.setStyleHint(QFont::TypeWriter);
originalEditor->setFont(monoFont);
parsedEditor->setFont(monoFont);

In updatePlayAlongLayoutDensity() CSS, replace the hardcoded font name:

cpp
"font-family: '" + m_currentFont + "', 'Courier New', monospace;"

And same in runInitialParse() CSS.

Step 7 — Load saved font preference in constructor

cpp
// In MainWindow constructor, before setupLayout():
QSettings settings;
m_currentFont = settings.value("display/font", "Courier Prime").toString();

Load Order Note

The constructor order matters here — font must be loaded before setupLayout() creates the editors:

cpp
// Constructor order:
m_currentFont = settings.value("display/font", "Courier Prime").toString(); // 1st
setupMenus();    // 2nd
setupLayout();   // 3rd — uses m_currentFont for editor fonts
setupToolBar();  // 4th

That's the complete picture. Courier Prime as default, user picks from four bundled options via Preferences menu, choice persists across sessions via QSettings, and re-renders immediately on change. Clean, cross-platform, and a genuinely nice feature for your students and friends. 🎸

You said: Hi Claude, still need your eagle-eyes to check our code - latest source and logs are attached.

Hi Claude, still need your eagle-eyes to check our code - latest source and logs are attached... we get a crash but many updates at once that's not really surprising?? Thanks...

6:34 pm

Claude responded: Let me read the source file first before diagnosing!

Let me read the source file first before diagnosing!

Viewed 4 files

Found it! The crash is on line 976. Here's the exact problem in setupLayout():

cpp
originalEditor = new QPlainTextEdit(this);   // line 971 — created ✓
...
QFont monoFont(m_currentFont, 10);
monoFont.setStyleHint(QFont::TypeWriter);
originalEditor->setFont(monoFont);
parsedEditor->setFont(monoFont);             // line 976 — 💥 CRASH: parsedEditor is still nullptr!

parsedEditor = new QTextEdit(this);          // line 980 — created too late!

parsedEditor->setFont(monoFont) is called before parsedEditor = new QTextEdit(this). It's a null pointer dereference — the font line was added above the constructor call when it needs to be below it.

Fix — swap the order so parsedEditor is created first:

cpp
void MainWindow::setupLayout() {
    // 1. Create BOTH widgets first
    originalEditor = new QPlainTextEdit(this);
    originalEditor->setPlaceholderText("Original File Content...");

    parsedEditor = new QTextEdit(this);          // ← move up here
    parsedEditor->setPlaceholderText("Parsed & Standardized Output...");
    parsedEditor->setReadOnly(true);

    // 2. Now apply font to both safely
    QFont monoFont(m_currentFont, 10);
    monoFont.setStyleHint(QFont::TypeWriter);
    originalEditor->setFont(monoFont);
    parsedEditor->setFont(monoFont);             // ← now safe

    // ... rest of function unchanged

That one ordering mistake is the entire crash. Everything else in the file looks correct. 🎸

You said: Wow.

Wow. Wow🚀🚀🎶🙏✔️- that's pretty cool😊.. now it looks and works great. Many thanks...

6:44 pm

Claude responded: That's fantastic news — so glad it's all coming together!

That's fantastic news — so glad it's all coming together! 🎉🎸

Looking at how far ChordLab has come in this session alone:

  • ✅ Crash-free and stable
  • ✅ Setlist buttons properly placed and enable/disable cleanly
  • ✅ Cross-platform path handling with getResourcesPath()
  • ✅ Four bundled fonts with user preference via Preferences menu
  • ✅ Font persisted across sessions via QSettings
  • ✅ Courier Prime as the default — much warmer than Consolas

And still on the roadmap when you're ready:

  • 🎯 Fine zoom (m_zoomCoarse + m_zoomFine) + column override
  • 🎯 CSS tightening to get closer to the SBPro layout density
  • 🎯 Per-song zoom/column preferences saved and restored
  • 🎯 Image/sheet music fragment support (longer term)
  • 🎯 macOS and Linux builds

It's shaping up into a genuinely useful app for your students and gig musicians. The fact that it's free and handles ChordPro with audio play-along is already more than most tools offer. 🎓

Come back whenever you're ready for the next round — enjoy playing with it in the meantime! 🎶

Claude responded: Great — let's do it!
